steps.action: The Taualuga is a traditional Samoan dance, cons with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: The Taualuga is a traditional Samoan dance, cons with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: This dance form has been adopted and adapted thr
steps.text: This dance form has been adopted and adapted throughout western Polynesia, most notably in Samoa, The Kingdom of Tonga, Uvea, Futuna, and Tokelau.

steps.action: The dance is frequently performed as the grand f
steps.text: The dance is frequently performed as the grand finale of an evening of entertainment or as the concluding number at Samoan wedding receptions, social functions, and other festivities.

steps.action: Traditionally, the Taualuga is performed by the
steps.text: Traditionally, the Taualuga is performed by the son or daughter of a chief.

steps.action: " However, Taupou in certain districts consist o
steps.text: " However, Taupou in certain districts consist of important individual female names or titles under Samoa's traditional social hierarchy and form of governance, the Fa'amatai chiefly system.

steps.action: This dance is sacred to the Samoan people and tr
steps.text: This dance is sacred to the Samoan people and traditionally only virgins were allowed to perform it.

steps.action: Today virginity is not necessarily a prerequisit
steps.text: Today virginity is not necessarily a prerequisite and although older adults and even teenage mothers are occasionally seen performing the final dance, a strong preference for unmarried performers is still the norm.

steps.action: The taupou was raised from youth in the arts of
steps.text: The taupou was raised from youth in the arts of hospitality, cultural rituals and ceremonies, chiefly protocol and demeanor, and the intricacies of the fine arts of dancing, the pinnacle of which was the taualuga.

steps.action: Conversely, it is common for a parishioner dress
steps.text: Conversely, it is common for a parishioner dressed as a taupou to dance and lead the procession in some Samoan Catholic congregations.

steps.action: On all other social occasions the taualuga is us
steps.text: On all other social occasions the taualuga is usually the last dance to be performed.

steps.action: This usually consisted of a traditional finely w
steps.text: This usually consisted of a traditional finely woven ‘ie toga mat, decorated with the prized feathers of the "sega" (collared lory or blue-crowned lorikeet), that was wrapped around the body.

steps.action: Today, most fine mats are bordered with dyed chi
steps.text: Today, most fine mats are bordered with dyed chicken feathers, far larger and less delicate than the traditional mats now seen only very rarely in Samoa, and only in museums and private collections abroad.

steps.action: Both the dancer and the mats were generously ano
steps.text: Both the dancer and the mats were generously anointed with scented coconut oil to give a cosmetic sheen.

steps.action: The crowning attire of the taupou or manaia was
steps.text: The crowning attire of the taupou or manaia was the traditional headdress known as the "tuiga.

steps.action: Today, the 'ie'ula is generally made of large co
steps.text: Today, the 'ie'ula is generally made of large colorful chicken feathers that are dyed any variety of hues besides the traditional red and white.

steps.action: This contemporary version of the ancient tuiga a
steps.text: This contemporary version of the ancient tuiga also uses materials that were never used traditionally, such as glass mirrors, faux jewels and pearls, plastic mesh and chicken feathers.

steps.action: In cases when the tuiga was not worn, the "pale
steps.text: In cases when the tuiga was not worn, the "pale fuiono" was usually worn with the "ie'ula" feather ornament tied to dangle from the back or side of the dancer's head.

steps.action: " While taupou and manaia did indeed dance and t
steps.text: " While taupou and manaia did indeed dance and twirl war clubs ("anava") when leading processions or concluding performances this segment should not be misconstrued as a component of the taualuga.

steps.action: When European whalers and entrepreneurs introduc
steps.text: When European whalers and entrepreneurs introduced the steel blubber knife and cane knife these blades were quickly adapted as warclubs and used for "ailao" dancing and warfare.

steps.action: The "ailao" is performed before the actual taual
steps.text: The "ailao" is performed before the actual taualuga as an exhibition of the dancer's skill, dexterity, grace, and coordination.

steps.action: It is symbolic of the former significance that t
steps.text: It is symbolic of the former significance that the taupou had in her role as the leader of ceremonial processions, dances, rituals, and war parties.

steps.action: The dance was accompanied by choral music and si
steps.text: The dance was accompanied by choral music and simple percussion such as the slit gong or rolled mats that were beaten with sticks.

steps.action: Singers, directed by a conductor known as "fuata
steps.text: Singers, directed by a conductor known as "fuataimi" or "fa'aluma," generally sat in rows or concentric semicircles around the dance area.

steps.action: The Samoan taualuga is known for its graceful re
steps.text: The Samoan taualuga is known for its graceful refinement, subtle hand and facial gestures, and the stately poise of the dancers movements and postures.
